title: Restoring chrome and metal fittings on vintage furniture
subtitle: Clean rust and pitting from chrome handles, escutcheons, and fittings on vintage furniture and re-polish to a working shine
excerpt: How to restore rusted or tarnished chrome and brass fittings on vintage furniture. Covers removing fittings, treating surface rust on chrome, polishing with fine abrasive, and re-lacquering to protect the restored finish.
sourceNotes: Traditional metal polishing and restoration practice. UK furniture restoration trade practice. City and Guilds Level 2 Furniture Restoration.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin layer of metallic chromium electrodeposited over a nickel-copper base layer on steel. The chromium layer is 0.2 to 0.5 microns thick on decorative furniture fittings (much thinner than industrial hard chrome. Pitting through the chrome layer exposes the nickel below, which then rusts. The chrome surface itself does not rust; the rust-coloured spots are nickel oxide showing through pits in the chrome. This means polishing the rust off removes the nickel layer and worsens the damage if continued past the surface scale.

content.text: Old handles and fittings clean up well with the right approach.

and brass fittings need different products: chrome takes a gentle cream polish; brass takes a slightly abrasive paste. Both must be removed from the furniture first so the polish cannot drip onto the wood finish.

Remove handles, hinges, and escutcheons from the piece before treating. Work on metal away from the timber: metal polishes contain solvents that lift finish from surrounding wood if they spill. Keep the screws in a labelled container.
Clean surface grease and loose scale
Wipe each fitting with white spirit on a lint-free cloth to remove grease, old polish, and fingerprint residue. Use a cotton swab for recesses. Allow to dry completely before polishing.

Apply a small amount of chrome or metal polish to a soft cloth and work in small circular strokes, not heavy linear pressure. Remove the polishing residue immediately with a clean cloth. On pitted chrome, the scale will lift but the pits will remain. This is the limit of polishing chrome without re-plating. A thin coat of clear lacquer applied after polishing seals the surface and slows re-oxidation.

Unlacquered brass polishes with Brasso or a similar mildly abrasive metal polish. Apply with a cloth and rub with moderate pressure until the patina lifts and the surface brightens. Work into mouldings with a soft brush. Rinse with warm water, dry thoroughly, and buff with a clean cloth. Lacquered brass must be stripped first: remove the old lacquer with acetone or lacquer thinner, then polish the bare brass before re-lacquering.

Apply a coat of clear metal lacquer in a spray can at 30 cm distance in light passes. Allow to dry fully before fitting. Handle the lacquered fittings in clean cotton gloves: fingerprints on fresh lacquer show in the cured finish.
